'''20th Century Studios''', alternatively '''Twentieth Century Studios''' and formerly named '''20th Century Fox''', is an American film production company that is located in southern California. The studio was involved in the development of the ''[[Star Wars]]'' [[Original trilogy]] and subsequently distributed the [[prequel trilogy]]. Located in the Century City area of Los Angeles, just west of Beverly Hills until Disney's lease with Fox Corporation ended and relocated to the Walt Disney Studios in Burbank, the studio is currently a subsidiary of [[The Walt Disney Company]].<ref name="Disney" /> In January, 2020, it was reported that Disney would be renaming the company 20th Century Studios to avoid confusion with [[Wikipedia:Fox Corporation|Fox Corporation]], which owns [[Wikipedia:21st Century Fox|21st Century Fox]] former news and sports assets.<ref name="Variety rename">{{WebCite|url=https://variety.com/2020/film/news/disney-dropping-fox-20th-century-studios-1203470349|text=Disney Drops Fox Name, Will Rebrand as 20th Century Studios, Searchlight Pictures|author=Adam Vary|date=2020-01-17|work=[[Wikipedia:Variety (magazine)|''Variety'']]|archivedate=20200130202447}}</ref><ref name="NYT rename">{{WebCite|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2020/01/17/business/media/disney-fox-name.html|text=Disney Drops Fox From Names of Studios It Bought From Rupert Murdoch|author=Brooke Barnes|date=2020-01-17|work=[[Wikipedia:The New York Times|''The New York Times'']]|archivedate=20200204032118}}</ref>

==History==
===Relationship with Lucasfilm, 1977–2019===
[[George Lucas]]' original deal with Fox gave the latter some merchandising rights and copyrights. [[Star Wars: Episode IV A New Hope|''Star Wars'': Episode IV ''A New Hope'''s]] copyright was originally owned by Fox<ref name="Catalog">{{BookCite|title=Catalog of Copyright Entries - Motion Pictures (January-June 1977)|version=Third Series|number=Vol. 31, Pts. 12-13, N. 1|publisher=Copyright Office, [[Wikipedia:Library of Congress|Library of Congress]]|date=[[1977]]|issn=0090-8371|link=https://archive.org/details/1977motionpictur3311213libr/page/n98/mode/1up}}</ref> and the merchandising rights were split 60/40 between Fox and Lucas. Fox's ''Star Wars'' IP and licensing rights, except ''A New Hope''{{'s}} copyright, reverted to Lucasfilm on [[September 1]], [[1981]].<ref name="luckystars97">{{WebCite|url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1997-02-06-fi-25849-story.html|text=By George, He Can Thank His Lucky 'Stars'|date=1997-02-06|work=[[Wikipedia:Los Angeles Times|''Los Angeles Times'']]|quote=Pollock negotiated for a 60-40 split of merchandising between Fox and Lucas to begin with; the percentage diminished over two years until all rights reverted to Lucas.|archivedate=20200807151245}}</ref><ref name="MakingOfRotJ">''[[The Making of Return of the Jedi]]''</ref><ref name="Copyright1">{{WebCite|url=https://cocatalog.loc.gov/cgi-bin/Pwebrecon.cgi?DB=local&PAGE=First&Search_Arg=Star+Wars+brochure+%26+132+other+titles.&Search_Code=TALL|text=Star Wars brochure & 132 other titles.|work=[[Wikipedia:Copyright Catalog|Copyright Catalog]]|archivedate=20210423103615}}</ref><ref name="Trademark1">{{WebCite|url=https://assignments.uspto.gov/assignments/q?db=tm&reel=0407&frame=0551|text=Trademark Assignment Details|work=[[Wikipedia:United States Patent and Trademark Office|U.S. Trademark Database]]|archivedate=20210423103918}}</ref><ref name="trademark2">{{WebCite|url=https://assignments.uspto.gov/assignments/q?db=tm&reel=0407&frame=F551|text=Trademark Assignment Details (Alderaan)|work=[[Wikipedia:United States Patent and Trademark Office|U.S. Trademark Database]]|archivedate=20210423104149}}</ref> Lucasfilm eventually gained ''A New Hope''{{'s}} copyright on [[January 18]], [[1997]].<ref name="Copyright2">{{WebCite|url=https://cocatalog.loc.gov/cgi-bin/Pwebrecon.cgi?DB=local&PAGE=First&Search_Arg=Star+Wars+%26+1+other+title&Search_Code=TALL|text=Star Wars & 1 other title; By Twentieth Century Fox Film Corporation.|work=[[Wikipedia:Copyright Catalog|Copyright Catalog]]|archivedate=20210423104447}}</ref><ref name="Copyright3">{{WebCite|url=https://cocatalog.loc.gov/cgi-bin/Pwebrecon.cgi?DB=local&PAGE=First&Search_Arg=RE0000925302&Search_Code=REGS|text=Star wars / by Twentieth Century Fox Film Corporation. (Renewal)|work=[[Wikipedia:Copyright Catalog|Copyright Catalog]]|archivedate=20210423104624}}</ref>

Despite the The Walt Disney Company's [[2012]] purchase of [[Lucasfilm]] and the release rights to all future ''Star Wars'' films, Fox was to retain original distribution rights to ''A New Hope'' in perpetuity in all media worldwide. Fox was also to retain theatrical, nontheatrical, and home video rights worldwide for the franchise's five subsequent films, which Lucasfilm produced and financed independently, through [[May]] [[2020]], at which time ownership was to transfer to Disney. This complex relationship between Disney and Fox, particularly in regards to Fox's perpetual rights to Episode IV, was to create an obstacle for any future boxed set comprising all nine films.<ref name="Hollywood Reporter">{{WebCite|url=https://www.hollywoodreporter.com/news/tangled-rights-could-tie-up-384541|text=Tangled Rights Could Tie Up Ultimate 'Star Wars' Box Set (Analysis)|author=Kim Masters|date=2012-10-30|work=[[Wikipedia:The Hollywood Reporter|''The Hollywood Reporter'']]|archivedate=20200108092132}}</ref>

===Disney acquisition, 2019–present===
In December 2017, [[The Walt Disney Company]] entered into negotiations with [[Wikipedia:21st Century Fox|21st Century Fox]] to acquire its film and television assets including 20th Century Fox. Though Disney had initially intended to purchase Fox for US$52.4 billion, Comcast posted a competing bid in June 2018, prompting Disney to raise their bid to US$71.3 billion. Following protracted bidding, negotiations, and clearances from regulatory bodies, Disney purchased 21st Century Fox's film and television assets including 20th Century Fox on March 20, 2019. As part of the acquisition, Disney also acquired the rights to the [[Original trilogy]] and the [[Prequel trilogy]]. In addition, Disney also acquired the [[Wikipedia:The Simpsons|''The Simpsons'']], Futurama, [[Star Wars]], [[Wikipedia:X-Men|''X-Men'']], [[Wikipedia:Fantastic Four|''Fantastic Four'']], [[Wikipedia: Avatar (2009 film)|''Avatar'']], [[Wikipedia:Planet of the Apes|''Planet of the Apes'']], [[Wikipedia:Ice Age (franchise)|''Ice Age'']], [[Wikipedia:Rio (franchise)|''Rio'']], and [[Wikipedia:Anastasia (1997 film)|''Anastasia'']] franchises. Fox's news and sports assets were spun into a separate company called the [[Wikipedia:Fox Corporation|Fox Corporation]].<ref name="Hollywood Reporter Fox Deal">{{WebCite|url=https://www.hollywoodreporter.com/news/disney-closes-fox-deal-creating-global-content-powerhouse-1174498|text=Disney Closes $71.3 Billion Fox Deal, Creating Global Content Powerhouse|author=George Szalai & Paul Bond|date=2019-03-19|work=[[Wikipedia:The Hollywood Reporter|''The Hollywood Reporter'']]|archivedate=20191106071015}}</ref><ref name="Digital Trends Disney-Fox deal">{{WebCite|url=https://www.digitaltrends.com/movies/disney-21st-century-fox-merger|text=Disney's Deadpool? Netflix worries? What Disney's billion-dollar Fox deal means|date=2019-03-19|work=[[Wikipedia:Digital Trends|''Digital Trends'']]|archivedate=20191221003613}}</ref>

On January 17, 2020, it was reported that Disney would be renaming the company 20th Century Studios to avoid confusion with the Fox Corporation.<ref name="Variety rename" /><ref name="NYT rename" />
